content.full_text:
Law Offices of
OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH,

May 29 3 35 PM '90

May 29, 1990

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Formal Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

This firm represents Cella Barr Associates
("CBA"). This letter is a request for formal written
advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) on
behalf of our client and the City of Riverbank
("City").

The request concerns the application of the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (California Government Code Section 87100 et seq.;
the "Act") to a proposed contract between Cella Barr
Associates ("CBA") and the City of Riverbank. While
CBA has been chosen as the successful bidder on the
contract, final approval and execution of the contract
has not yet occurred. The award of the contract is
being held in abeyance pending your response to this
request.

The proposed contract involves preparation of
studies and plans preliminary to the adoption of a
Specific Plan by the City for a 600-acre unincorporated
area adjacent to the city limits ("Planning Area"). A
concern has been raised regarding a possible conflict
of interest based on the fact that Ron Cheek, a CBA
employee and its local district manager, has provided
and continues to provide civil engineering and related
services to Lloyd Glasgow. Mr. Glasgow has an
ownership interest in property located in the Planning
Area. We believe that no disqualification under
the Political Reform Act would be required as a result
of the proposed contract, and we therefore present this
request.

LANCE H. OLSON
BRUCE J. HAGEL
LEROY Y. FONG
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH
GEORGE M. WATERS
DIANE M. FISHBURN

OF COUNSEL
LLOYD G. CONNELLY, Member
California State Legislature

300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, California 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 442-2952
FAX: (916) 442-1280

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 29 1990
Page Two

QUESTIONS

1. Would CBA employees be "public officials" within the
meaning of Government Code sections 87100 and 87103 when
providing services pursuant to the proposed contract?

2. If CBA employees are "public officials," what
governmental decisions are they "participating" in within the
meaning of Government Code section 87100?

3. To the extent CBA employees are "participating" in
governmental decisions, under what circumstances would
disqualification would be required?

FACTS

1. Entities and Individuals

The facts concerning the various entities and individuals
involved in this request for advice are as follows:

CBA. CBA is a private consulting firm with approximately
140 employees in offices in Arizona and California. It provides
planning services as well as consulting services in engineering,
surveying, landscape architecture, and hydrology to public and
private entities. CBA's customers include numerous public
entities, both in California and in other states. CBA is a
closely held corporation and has approximately 50,000 outstanding
shares.

Don Laidlaw. Mr. Laidlaw is a vice-president of CBA and
owns approximately 3.4% of the total outstanding shares. He will
be the Project Manager for this contract. Other CBA professional
staff include [names of CBA staff]. These CBA staff will be
involved, as well as several subcontractors selected by CBA.
None of these individuals owns a 10 percent or greater interest
in CBA. They may have any of these individuals provided services
directly to Lloyd Glasgow.

Ron Cheek. Mr. Cheek is a vice president of CBA and the
district manager for CBA's office in Manteca. He is a CBA
employee; he has no ownership interest in CBA. Ron Cheek will
not be involved in any aspect of the contract with Riverbank. In
addition, his income from CBA will not be tied to the contract in
any way. Mr. Cheek provides on-going civil engineering and
related services to Mr. Glasgow and to entities in which Mr.
Glasgow has an ownership interest. None of these services

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 29, 1990
Page Three

relate to property located in the Planning Area. If the contract
between CBA and the City is executed, he will not be performing
any services for Lloyd Glasgow relative to the plan which is the
subject of the contract.

CBA's Relationship with Lloyd Glasgow. Mr. Glasgow is a
land developer who has done business both as a sole owner
(Liberty Developments) and as a partner in a business entity
(Gruen Winters) in which he has a one-third interest. Mr.
Glasgow and CBA have had an on-going business relationship for
several years. In the past, Mr. Cheek has performed services in
connection with several residential subdivisions in the City for
Liberty Developments and Gruen Winters. These services include
preparing tentative and final subdivision maps, requests for
annexations and pre-zoning, general plan amendments, as well as
providing related civil engineering services.

Mr. Cheek has provided professional services to Mr. Glasgow
with respect to two subdivisions located in the vicinity of the
Planning Area: the Wallace Viney annexation and the Morrill North
annexation. The Morrill North subdivision is located directly
across the street from the northern boundary of the Planning
Area.

Mr. Glasgow owns or has an interest in approximately 120 to
150 acres in the 600-acre Planning Area. (A map of the Planning
Area is enclosed.) Obviously, none of this area is zoned, and
the land is currently undeveloped. The total number of property
owners in the Planning Area is approximately fifteen to twenty.
If the Specific Plan is adopted by the City and the sphere of
influence determination is approved by LAFCo, property owners in
the Planning Area may proceed with development of the property.

2. Proposed Contract with City of Riverbank.

Under the proposed contract, CBA will conduct some of the
preliminary studies and oversee the preparation of other studies
by subcontractors. (A copy of the proposed contract is
enclosed.) CBA will also develop the draft Specific Plan itself
and the draft EIR. The contract provides that CBA's services
will extend through the certification of the final EIR and
adoption of the Specific Plan by the Riverbank City Council.

The planning process is divided into three stages. Stages I
and II involve the technical support studies and the policy
development for the various Specific Plan Alternatives. The
culmination of Stage II will be the selection of the Preferred
Plan Alternative; this decision will be made by the City Manager

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 29, 1990
Page Four

with input from city planning and public works staff, CBA and
its subcontractors (the "Consultant Team") of the Planning
Commission and consideration of comments from interested parties.
Stage III will consist of developing the Preferred Plan
Alternative into the draft Specific Plan and preparing the
accompanying draft Environmental Impact Report ("EIR"). The draft
Specific Plan and draft EIR will be formally presented by City
staff with CBA support to the Planning Commission for
recommendation and ultimately to the City Council for adoption.

The contract generally provides that CBA and the other
members of the Consultant Team will closely consult and
coordinate with City staff and other public agencies. It also
proposes that CBA will coordinate several public workshops on
various aspects of the studies and the alternative plans.

DISCUSSION

Our first question asks whether CBA employees who will be
performing the services required by the proposed contract are
"public officials" within the meaning of the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act. (Government Code
sections 87100 and 87103; all subsequent statutory references are
to the Government Code.) The definition of "public official" in
Section 82047 expressly includes every consultant to a local or
state governmental agency.

FPPC Regulation 18700(a)(2) further defines "consultant" to
include any natural person who provides, under contract,
information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a governmental
agency except a person who:

--   Conducts research and arrives at conclusions with
respect to his or her rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel independent of the control and
direction of the agency or of any agency official, other than
normal contract monitoring; and

--   Possesses no authority with respect to any agency
decision beyond the rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel.

Pursuant to this regulation, the FPPC has generally
distinguished between individuals who perform on-going services
to government agencies which involve the exercise of professional
judgment and those individuals who are hired to provide technical
or professional services for a specific project or study. The

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 29, 1990
Page Five

first type is a "public official" covered by the Act (see e.g.,
Mahoney Opinion, 3 FPPC opinions 69, No. 76-082, August 17, 1977;
Leidigh Advice Letter, No. A-89-320); the latter type is not
subject to the Act (see e.g., Crisis Advice Letter, No. A-82-029;
Crough Advice Letter, No. A-83-069; and Torres Advice Letter, No.
86-245.)

With respect to other types of situations which may involve
an individual hired for a specific project but who interacts
closely with the agency such as the present situation, the FPPC
has focussed on whether the individual "participate" in the
agency decisions. It appears that a key factor in the staff's
analysis has been the extent to which the individual interacts
with the governmental agency staff. For example, in the recent
Corn Advice Letter (No. I-89-433), the staff reasoned as follows:

In providing the design for the improvements
which are the subject of the benefit
assessment, the engineer may work independent
of the control and direction of the public
agency engineer governed only by the terms of
the contract. In such circumstances, the
engineer is not deemed to be a consultant and
is not, therefore, subject to the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act.

On the other hand, in providing the design
for the improvements which are the subject of
the benefit assessment, if the engineer works
with and discusses the proposed design with
the public agency engineer, thus giving the
public agency engineer the opportunity to
comment upon, and provide input regarding,
the proposed design for the improvements, the
engineer would be deemed a consultant. Under
these circumstances, the engineer is not
working independent of the control and
direction of the public agency engineer
within the meaning of Regulation 18700(a)(2).
Similarly, when the engineer is determining
the estimated costs of the proposed
improvements, and when he is developing
the methodology and performing the calculations
for spreading the benefit within the assessment district,
the engineer is not working independent of
the control and direction of the public

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 29, 1990
Page Six

agency engineer if the is seeking input and
comments form the public agency engineer
regarding these tasks. Under such
circumstances, the engineer is acting much
like an employee of the agency who is under
the supervision of the public agency
engineer. In that situation, the engineer is
deemed a consultant and, therefore, a public
official within the meaning of the Act.

Here, while the contract involves a discrete project, CBA
employees will clearly be in frequent contact with the City
planning and public works staff during the planning process. The
CBA employees will not make the decision on the Preferred Plan
Alternative nor will they make any other decisions concerning the
actual contents of the draft Specific Plan or any of the
decisions relating to the certification of the environmental
documentation. However, as experts in the various fields, the
CBA employees will be preparing studies and making substantive
recommendations to the City staff and officials concerning these
documents. While we believe that most of the contact between
city staff and CBA employees falls within the exception in FPPC
Regulation 18700(a)(2) for "normal contract monitoring," it
appears that the FPPC may well conclude based on the above cited
advice letters that the CBA employees are "consultants" covered
by the conflict of interest statutes.

We note, however, the City has already made the major
governmental decisions with respect to undertaking the planning
process for purposes of proceeding toward annexation; these are
decisions which CBA did not participate in and which CBA cannot
affect in any way.

Assuming that you determine that the CBA employees are
consultants by the Act, the next question is whether they would
have any potential conflicts on interest based on their financial
interest in CBA or on CBA's relationship with Mr. Glasgow.

Since none of the CBA employees involved has a 10% or
greater ownership interest in CBA, none of CBA's clients are a
"source of income" within the meaning of section 87101(e) and
section 82030. (See also Moe Advice Letter, No. A-89-454.) This
situation is distinguishable from the situation presented in the
Russell Advice Letter (No. A-88-484) because none of the CBA
employees has a greater than 10% ownership interest in CBA.

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 29, 1990
Page Seven

Accordingly, none of the CBA employees would be required to
disqualify themselves on any planning decisions which could
materially affect any CBA clients including Lloyd Glasgow. We
ask that you confirm this conclusion in your response.

With respect to CBA itself, it is equally clear that CBA is
a "source of income" of more than $250 to each of the CBA
employees pursuant to section 87103(c). In addition, since they
are employees of CBA, they may not make any decisions which would
foreseeably and materially affect CBA under section 87103(d).
For Don Laidlaw, his investment interest in CBA would similarly
require disqualification under section 87103(a) with respect to
decisions which would foreseeably and materially affect CBA.

CBA would be materially affected if, as a result of the
decisions made pursuant to the contract, CBA received additional
business. Thus the critical issues here are which decisions are
the CBA employees "participating" in with the meaning of Section
87100 and whether it is reasonably foreseeable that these
decisions will result in CBA's receiving additional business.

Since Mr. Glasgow is an established client of CBA, We
believe that it is likely that CBA will be retained by Lloyd
Glasgow to provide services in connection with the development of
his property in the planning area after the Specific Plan is
adopted and the area annexed. While Mr. Glasgow has not actually
retained CBA or even discussed this with Mr. Cheek, CBA has
provided similar services to Mr. Glasgow in the past and
continues to do so in the present. Thus it is reasonably
foreseeable that Mr. Glasgow will continue to retain CBA for
services in the future. At the very least, CBA has reasonable
expectations that Mr. Glasgow will do so. However, it is our
view that none of the decisions which will be made in the
planning process can affect whether Mr. Glasow continues as CBA's
client. The decisions may affect the type of work CBA does but
not the overall relationships.

In this regard, we believe that the 1989 Moe Advice Letter,
supra, which involved a similar situation, aids in the resolution
of the question. In that letter to the City of Roseville, a law
firm was being considered as the contract advisor to a joint
powers authority for the construction of four freeway
interchanges; one of the firm's clients owned property in the
area to be benefited by the freeway interchanges. With respect
to the lawyer who did not have any ownership interest in the law
firm, the Commission determined that it was not reasonably
foreseeable that the decisions to be made by the joint powers

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 29, 1990
Page Eight

authority would affect the law firm, reasoning as follows:

"The authority does not control changes in
land use entitlement or other matters on
which [the law firm] represents [its client].
"The land use entitlements sought by [the
client] are not dependent upon the
interchanges. The volume of business [the
law firm] receives from [its clients] will
not be affected by the decisions of the
authority, because the major decisions
regarding construction and location of the
interchanges have already been made."

We feel the analysis in the Moe letter applies to the
factual situation presented here. The fundamental major
decisions to initiate the planning process and proceed toward
annexation of the Planning Area have already been made by the
City. CBA employees clearly did not participate in any of these
decisions. These are major governmental policy decisions which
have had the greatest impact on the property owners. The
presumption of the proposed contract work plan are that a plan will be adopted and the area annexed. While
these decisions are not irrevocable by the City, they will not be
changed by the planning process set forth in the proposed
contract. CBA has been selected to assist only in the
implementation of these decisions.

This situation is not unlike what occurs in every city and
county in its land use planning activities. The agency makes the
decisions to proceed with an annexation or other planning effort.
Outside experts are usually hired to provide the technical and
scientific expertise necessary to comply with the requirements of
state environmental and planning laws. While these experts are
obviously hired for their professional expertise, the agency does
not usually relinquish its basic control and responsibility for
the planning process. In many cases, as in the present
situation, city planning and public works staff substantively
review all of the consultants' work and also provide the policy
direction and recommendations to the agency. There is valid
public concern regarding the extent to which local agencies may
be limited in their ability to conduct their required planning if
many outside experts are disqualified based on their employment
by private entities.

Certainly lines must be drawn which protect the public
interest in preventing decisions biased by private financial
interests. However, these lines should also take into account

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 29, 1990
Page Nine

the public interest in obtaining the best contractors
for governmental projects. We think the FPPC drew an appropriate
line in this regard in the Moe Advice Letter, and we ask that you
consider applying that line in this situation.

While the planning process necessarily encompasses many
decisions concerning the content of the Specific Plan, these
decisions will not affect whether CBA receives additional
business from Mr. Glasgow. Many of these decisions are
constrained within the limits imposed by the standards and
policies in the General Plan, state law, and other goals and
policies for the process already set by the City. The realistic
alternatives for most of these decisions are few. Moreover, many
of the decisions involve development guidelines and other similar
details which could not possibly affect whether CBA would
ultimately receive increased or decreased business from Mr.
Glasgow.

There are some specific planning decisions which may affect
the type of development which will be allowed in the Planning
Area, including Mr. Glasgow's property. For example, some of the
choices which must be made include decisions on the proposed
zoning for the Planning Area. These types of decisions may have
significant implications for property owners including Mr.
Glasgow. However, it is difficult to see how these decisions
will materially affect the volume of business Mr. Glasgow brings
to CBA. Whether or not a particular Riverbank project is
developed as housing, shopping, commercial or individual
affect the business relationship between CBA and Mr. Glasgow nor
the volume of business which flows from Mr. Glasgow's numerous
other projects to CBA. If the Specific Plan is not adopted or
does not allow for the development of Mr. Glasgow's property
consistent with his business objectives, it is reasonably
foreseeable Mr. Glasgow will continue his development projects at
other locations, and continue to employee CBA to provide
engineering services.

We ask for your specific guidance in this regard. If, in
your opinion, the potential conflicts of interest for the CBA
employees are substantial, it is in the interest of both and the
City to reconsider the proposed contract. On the other hand, if
you agree that the potential for conflicts is minimal, the City
and CBA can proceed toward finalization of the contract. Any
suggestions you would have to further minimize any potential for
conflicts would be welcomed.

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 29, 1990
Page Ten

CONCLUSION

In conclusion, we believe that the CBA employees do not have
any conflicts of interest under the Act with respect to the
proposed contract. While we realize the Commission may determine
that the employees are "consultants" covered by the Act, we
believe neither CBA's business relationship with Mr. Glasgow nor
the "nonaffluent" interest in CBA would require disqualification
in this situation.

Because time is of the essence, I ask that you review this
letter and provide your response as soon as possible. I am
available to meet with the staff attorney assigned to the letter
in order to facilitate your review of this request.

Please feel free to contact either of the undersigned if you
wish additional information or you would like to discuss the
matter.

Very truly yours,

OLSON CONNELLY, HAGEL,
FONG & LEIDIGH

[signature]
DIANE M. FISHBURN

DMF:sk
Enclosures as stated
cc: Tom Hallinan
    Don Laidlaw

sections.question:
1. Would CBA employees be "public officials" within the meaning of Government Code sections 87100 and 87103 when providing services pursuant to the proposed contract?
2. If CBA employees are "public officials," what governmental decisions are they "participating" in within the meaning of Government Code section 87100?
3. To the extent CBA employees are "participating" in governmental decisions, under what circumstances would disqualification be required?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether employees of Cella Barr Associates (CBA), a private consulting firm contracted by the City of Riverbank to prepare planning studies and a Specific Plan for a 600-acre area, would be considered "public officials" under the Political Reform Act, and if so, whether disqualification would be required given that a CBA employee provides services to a property owner with interests in the planning area.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The document indicates that CBA employees would be deemed consultants and thus public officials under the Political Reform Act because they will work closely with city planning and public works staff, providing input and receiving comments, rather than working independently. However, the conclusion section appears to be truncated in the provided text.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is truncated mid-discussion section. Questions and facts sections are complete and clear. The conclusion/short answer section is not present in the provided text. The FPPC's response reasoning is partially visible in the Discussion section, but the final conclusion is cut off. Date is May 29, 1990; document ID is 90386.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Would CBA employees be "public officials" within the meaning of Government Code sections 87100 and 87103 when providing services pursuant to the proposed contract?
2. If CBA employees are "public officials," what governmental decisions are they "participating" in within the meaning of Government Code section 87100?
3. To the extent CBA employees are "participating" in governmental decisions, under what circumstances would disqualification be required?

CONCLUSION: The document indicates that CBA employees would be deemed consultants and thus public officials under the Political Reform Act because they will work closely with city planning and public works staff, providing input and receiving comments, rather than working independently. However, the conclusion section appears to be truncated in the provided text.

embedding.summary: This is a request for formal FPPC advice regarding potential conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act for a private consulting firm (CBA) contracted by the City of Riverbank to prepare planning studies and a Specific Plan, given that one CBA employee provides services to a property owner with interests in the planning area.
